topic: SK Hynix lists on Nasdaq in major foreign listing
summary: SK Hynix has raised $26.5bn in its US IPO and listed on the Nasdaq stock exchange, selling 177.9 million American depositary shares at $149 each. The company's shares have surged as much as 17% in their first day of trading. SK Hynix's listing marks the largest-ever by a foreign company in the US. The company's shares are currently trading on the Nasdaq.

summary_sources.title: South Korea's SK Hynix raises $26.5bn in record-breaking US IPO
AI chip fever returns? SK Hynix share price surges 13% after blockbuster $26.5 billion US listing | Stock Market News
Chip giant SK Hynix raises $26.5bn as shares surge in bumper US listing

sections.paragraph: SK Hynix, a South Korean memory chip giant, has entered the exclusive ranks of companies worth at least $1 trillion, driven by the explosive demand for semiconductors used in AI. The company's share price has skyrocketed, with a 240 percent increase since the start of the year and over 80 percent this month alone. SK Hynix made a strong debut on the Nasdaq, raising $26.5 billion through its American listing, which marks the largest-ever listing by a foreign company in the US. The company's success is part of a broader AI-driven rally in South Korea's stock market, which has seen significant gains. As the world's second-largest memory chipmaker, SK Hynix has emerged as one of the biggest beneficiaries of the AI-driven demand for advanced memory chips used in data centers and AI applications. The company's stellar run has been fueled by investor appetite for AI-linked semiconductor stocks, despite recent corrections in the sector.

sources.title: South Korea’s SK Hynix enters exclusive $1 trillion club | Business and Economy | Al Jazeera
AI chip fever returns? SK Hynix share price surges 13% after blockbuster $26.5 billion US listing | Stock Market News
South Korea's SK Hynix raises $26.5bn in record-breaking US IPO

sections.paragraph: SK Hynix's listing on the Nasdaq stock exchange has made a significant impact, raising a record-breaking $26.5bn in its initial public offering (IPO), the largest-ever listing by a foreign company in the US. This surpasses Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba's $25bn debut in 2014 and ranks as the second-largest globally, after SpaceX's record-breaking $85.7bn Nasdaq listing in June. The listing is seen as a reflection of the shift in investor sentiment towards companies in the semiconductor supply chain, with SK Hynix's stellar run mirroring a broader AI-driven rally in South Korea's stock market. However, despite the surge in the Kospi index, which has risen about 80% this year to record highs, investors remain divided over whether the cheap valuations present a buying opportunity or reflect concerns over the sustainability of the AI-driven memory boom. The listing has also sparked concerns over market volatility due to the heavy concentration in chip stocks, with Finance Minister Koo Yun-cheol warning of increasing market volatility. SK Hynix's IPO has sold 177.9 million American depositary shares (ADS) at $149 each, equivalent to 18 million ordinary shares.

sources.title: South Korea's SK Hynix raises $26.5bn in record-breaking US IPO

sections.paragraph: The listing of SK Hynix on the US market has significant economic implications, with the company raising $26.5bn in its New York share offering, the largest ever listing by a foreign firm in the US. This move is expected to support the development of high-end chips, with plans to build plants in Korea and potentially benefiting from US investment. However, some experts, such as Hanyang University business professor Yun Youngjin, note that a Nasdaq listing carries risks, including the potential for investors to move money away from South Korea's stock market. The South Korean government has unveiled plans for over $880bn of investments in partnership with SK Hynix and Samsung, and the listing has already had a positive impact on the benchmark Kospi index, which has risen by over 70% this year. The success of SK Hynix's listing, with shares surging as much as 17% on their first day of trading, is seen as a boost to the industry, driven by the growing demand for AI chips.
